stem: For each \( n \ge 1 \), define \( f_n(x) = x^{n} \) on \( [0,1] \). This sequence of functions converges POINTWISE on \( [0,1] \) (fix \( x \), then let \( n \to \infty \)) but, because the pointwise limit jumps at \( x=1 \), not uniformly. What is the pointwise limit \( \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} f_n(x) \) at \( x = \dfrac{[[num]]}{[[den]]} \)?
solution: Fix \( x = [[num]]/[[den]] \), which lies in \( [0,1) \) since \( [[num]] < [[den]] \). Letting \( n \to \infty \) in \( f_n(x) = x^{n} \), the geometric-style decay sends \( x^{n} \to 0 \) whenever \( 0 \le x < 1 \) (unlike at \( x=1 \), where every \( f_n(1)=1 \)). So the pointwise limit here is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the pointwise limit only AT \( x=1 \); here \( x<1 \), so \( x^n \) decays to \( 0 \) instead.
This mistakes the point \( x \) itself for the limit of \( x^n \).
This inverts the fraction defining \( x \).
This is an arbitrary constant unrelated to \( x^{n} \)'s actual limiting behavior.
This adds the numerator and denominator of \( x \) instead of evaluating the limit.
This uses the reciprocal of the denominator alone, unrelated to \( x^n \)'s limiting behavior.

stem: For each \( n \ge 1 \), define \( f_n(x) = n x (1-x)^{n} \) on \( [0,1] \). This sequence converges POINTWISE on \( [0,1] \) (fix \( x \), then let \( n \to \infty \)) but not uniformly — the peak of \( f_n \) does not shrink to \( 0 \) even though every fixed point does. What is the pointwise limit \( \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} f_n(x) \) at \( x = \dfrac{[[num]]}{[[den]]} \)?
solution: Fix \( x = [[num]]/[[den]] \in (0,1) \), so \( 0 < 1-x < 1 \). As \( n \to \infty \), the geometric factor \( (1-x)^{n} \) decays exponentially, which beats the linear growth of \( nx \); the product \( \to 0 \). The pointwise limit here is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the point \( x \) itself, ignoring that the exponential factor \( (1-x)^n \) forces the whole product to \( 0 \).
This wrongly assumes the product approaches \( 1 \).
This uses \( 1-x \) itself instead of recognizing \( (1-x)^n \to 0 \) dominates the linear factor \( nx \).
This reports the numerator of \( x \) alone, unrelated to the actual limiting behavior.
This reports the reciprocal of the denominator of \( x \), unrelated to the actual limiting behavior.

stem: For each \( n \ge 1 \), define \( f_n(x) = \dfrac{nx + 1}{n + x} \) for \( x \in \mathbb{R} \). This sequence converges POINTWISE on \( \mathbb{R} \) (fix \( x \), then let \( n \to \infty \)). What is the pointwise limit \( \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} f_n(x) \) at \( x = \dfrac{[[num]]}{[[den]]} \)?
solution: Fix \( x = [[num]]/[[den]] \) and divide numerator and denominator of \( \dfrac{nx+1}{n+x} \) by \( n \): \( \dfrac{x + 1/n}{1 + x/n} \to \dfrac{x + 0}{1 + 0} = x = [[answer]] \). (The pointwise limit function is simply \( f(x) = x \).)
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes the ratio of leading coefficients (both equal to \( n \)) is \( 1 \) regardless of \( x \), ignoring that \( x \) itself IS the pointwise limit here.
This wrongly assumes the whole expression vanishes.
This adds the numerator and denominator of \( x \) instead of just returning \( x \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
